claim: Core job: automatic timesheet collection and payroll calculation factoring in rate and holiday bonuses.
quote: We wanted the shift timesheet to be collected automatically, for payroll to be calculated automatically factoring in the rate and holiday bonuses

claim: Part of the job statement — taking the schedule-dispatcher role off himself, so payroll is calculated without errors.
quote: For payroll to be calculated without errors and for me not to be a schedule dispatcher in DMs

claim: Specific trigger — underpaying barista Kate for two shifts due to a missed WhatsApp message.
quote: There was a specific case — we underpaid one barista, Kate, for two shifts, because she logged them in WhatsApp and I just didn't scroll far enough in the chat and missed it

claim: Emotional reaction to the incident — shame toward the employee.
quote: I felt genuinely ashamed, honestly, because the person had worked, and I basically almost shortchanged her pay just because of the mess

claim: Immediate action right after the trigger — message to the partner about the need to change the system.
quote: That's when I wrote to my partner that we needed to do something about this, we couldn't keep going like that

claim: Job map: search for services started with Google and the coffee shop owners' chat.
quote: We started googling, asked in a chat for coffee shop owners, they threw us like five services

claim: Rollout happened one location at a time.
quote: About two weeks, I think, we onboarded one location at a time, so as not to break everything at once

claim: Friction at rollout start — older baristas got confused during the first week.
quote: Two of the older baristas genuinely got confused the first week, called me with questions ten times a day

claim: Execution failure — incorrect night shift calculation at one location.
quote: Also the night shift didn't calculate correctly right away at one location, we had to fix it manually once

claim: Current/prior solution — three years of Excel plus shift lists in WhatsApp.
quote: Well look, we ran everything in Excel for like three years plus shift lists in WhatsApp, baristas would send photos of the timesheet

claim: Workaround — Google Sheet with formulas, abandoned when scaling to the third location.
quote: We tried a Google Sheet with formulas for a while, my partner made it himself, but it fell apart on us when we added the third location, the formulas broke

claim: Workaround — shifts module in a checklist app, abandoned due to an overloaded interface.
quote: Baristas complained that there were five screens just to check in for a shift, they eventually just stopped bothering, went back to WhatsApp anyway

claim: Desired outcome — baristas see their schedule on their own phone, without DMs to the owner.
quote: We also wanted baristas to see their own schedule on their phone, instead of asking me in DMs for the tenth time

claim: Desired outcome — employees trust the payroll calculations.
quote: I also wanted baristas to trust the calculations, so there wouldn't be a feeling like they were being cheated

claim: Adjacent job — vacation planning got simpler, previously kept in a notebook.
quote: Yeah, actually, planning vacations got simpler, before it used to be a separate nightmare in a notebook

claim: Emotional job — relief from no longer fearing new payroll mistakes.
quote: And also, honestly, I started sleeping better, because I stopped being afraid that I'd messed up someone's pay again somewhere

claim: Emotional job — didn't want to look like a dishonest employer in employees' eyes.
quote: Yes, exactly, I didn't want to look, in the baristas' eyes, like an owner who shortchanges them out of carelessness

claim: FORCES OF PROGRESS — push from the risk of errors outweighed the habit of WhatsApp after a repeat incident (unstable cell).
quote: After that he agreed we couldn't keep dragging it out, the risk of errors was more costly than the habit

claim: HABIT force — the partner suggested tolerating the old system for another six months.
quote: My partner even said, maybe forget it, let's stick with the old system for another six months, why break everything now

claim: ANXIETY force — fear that older employees wouldn't master the new app.
quote: Of course there was, I was afraid the baristas would refuse to bother with a new app, we've got people of different ages, some of them aren't into technology at all

claim: KEY UNMET OUTCOME — lack of employee trust in the accuracy of payroll calculation.
quote: I also wanted baristas to trust the calculations, so there wouldn't be a feeling like they were being cheated

claim: HIRING CRITERION — decided by the live demo with automatic calculation and geolocation, not the competitor's price.
quote: If I'm being completely honest, probably the live demo with the geolocation check-in and night shift calculation, that was the exact moment I understood

claim: JOB SEGMENT — owner of a small multi-location food service business, manually performing the role of schedule dispatcher and payroll calculator.
quote: Yes, exactly, so I wouldn't be a schedule secretary, I don't have the time for that as it is

claim: ADOPTION BARRIER — age gap in app adoption among part of the staff, confirmed in practice during the first week of rollout.
quote: Two of the older baristas genuinely got confused the first week, called me with questions ten times a day

claim: KEY INSIGHT: the customer 'hires' the product for reliable automatic tracking amid chain growth, but habit and anxiety about older employees slowed the decision until a specific underpayment incident made the risk of error more costly than the comfort of the old system.
quote: But back then it seemed like it'd hold up fine as is, until it all blew up with Kate
